snippets.title: Spotting growth classes by code shape
snippets.explanation: Single loop → O(n); nested loop over the same set → O(n²); halving → O(log n); halving + linear work per level → O(n log n).

snippets.title: Measuring growth empirically
snippets.code:
import time

for n in [1_000, 10_000, 100_000]:
    xs = list(range(n))

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    sum(xs)                          # O(n)
    t_linear = time.perf_counter() - t0

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    [x for a in xs[:1000] for x in xs[:1000] if a == x]   # O(n²)-ish slice
    t_quad = time.perf_counter() - t0

    print(f'n={n}  linear={t_linear:.4f}s  quadratic-ish={t_quad:.4f}s')
snippets.explanation: Multiply n by 10 → linear time multiplies by ~10, quadratic time by ~100. The doubling test is the simplest way to confirm a complexity class.

snippets.title: Right vs wrong data structure: O(n²) → O(n)
snippets.code:
stopwords_list = ['the', 'a', 'of', 'and']    # tiny here but pretend it's 10k
words = ['the', 'quick', 'brown', 'fox']

# Wrong — O(n_words * n_stopwords) = O(n*m)
filtered_slow = [w for w in words if w not in stopwords_list]

# Right — O(n_words) with set membership O(1)
stop = set(stopwords_list)
filtered_fast = [w for w in words if w not in stop]
snippets.explanation: Converting a list to a set once turns membership tests from O(m) per lookup into O(1).
